---
title: "Data Visualization for Exploratory Data Analysis"
author: "Sebastian Siedler, ssiedler3117@floridapoly.edu"
output: 
  html_document:
    keep_md: true
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
---

# Data Visualization Project 03


In this exercise you will explore methods to create different types of data visualizations (such as plotting text data, or exploring the distributions of continuous variables).


## PART 1: Density Plots

Using the dataset obtained from FSU's [Florida Climate Center](https://climatecenter.fsu.edu/climate-data-access-tools/downloadable-data), for a station at Tampa International Airport (TPA) for 2022, attempt to recreate the charts shown below which were generated using data from 2016. You can read the 2022 dataset using the code below: 

```{r, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(plotly)
weather_tpa <- read_csv("../data/tpa_weather_2022.csv")
# random sample
sample_n(weather_tpa, 4)
```

See Slides from Week 4 of Visualizing Relationships and Models (slide 10) for a reminder on how to use this type of dataset with the `lubridate` package for dates and times (example included in the slides uses data from 2016).

```{r, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(lubridate)

tpa_clean <- weather_tpa %>%
  unite("doy", year, month, day, sep = "-") %>%
  mutate(
    doy = ymd(doy),
    max_temp = as.double(max_temp),
    min_temp = as.double(min_temp),
    precipitation = as.double(precipitation)
  )

# Add month name as ordered factor for proper ordering in plots
tpa_clean <- tpa_clean %>%
  mutate(
    month_name = factor(month(doy, label = TRUE, abbr = FALSE),
      levels = month.name
    )
  )
```

## Exploratory Data Analysis — Tampa Weather Data

Before reproducing the assigned charts, a quick look at what the dataset contains.

```{r eda-tpa, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Dataset dimensions
cat("Rows:", nrow(tpa_clean), "\n")
cat("Date range:", format(min(tpa_clean$doy), "%Y-%m-%d"), "to",
    format(max(tpa_clean$doy), "%Y-%m-%d"), "\n")

# Missing or sentinel values
tpa_clean %>%
  summarise(
    n_missing_temp  = sum(is.na(max_temp) | max_temp == -99.9),
    n_missing_precip = sum(is.na(precipitation) | precipitation == -99.99),
    temp_min  = min(max_temp[max_temp != -99.9], na.rm = TRUE),
    temp_max  = max(max_temp[max_temp != -99.9], na.rm = TRUE),
    temp_mean = round(mean(max_temp[max_temp != -99.9], na.rm = TRUE), 1)
  )
```

The dataset covers all 365 days of 2022 from the Tampa International Airport station. A handful of observations have sentinel values (-99.9 for temperature, -99.99 for precipitation) representing missing measurements rather than actual readings; these are filtered out before each visualization. Daily maximum temperatures range from the low 50s °F in winter to the mid-90s °F in summer, with a mean around 83 °F. The most striking seasonal pattern is in precipitation, where the official rainy season (June–September) accounts for the majority of annual rainfall.

Using the 2022 data: 

(a) Create a plot like the one below:

```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width="80%", fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aalhamadani/dataviz_final_project/main/figures/tpa_max_temps_facet.png")
```

Hint: the option `binwidth = 3` was used with the `geom_histogram()` function.

```{r, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.align='center', out.width="80%", fig.alt="Faceted histogram of daily maximum temperatures in Tampa for each month of 2022, using a viridis color palette. Each panel shows the distribution for one month, revealing that summer months (June-September) are concentrated in the high 80s to low 90s Fahrenheit, while winter months are more spread across cooler ranges."}
tpa_clean %>%
  filter(!is.na(max_temp), max_temp != -99.9) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = max_temp, fill = month_name)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 3, show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~month_name, nrow = 3) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "viridis") +
  labs(
    x = "Maximum temperatures",
    y = "Number of Days"
  ) +
  theme_bw()
```

(b) Create a plot like the one below:

```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width="80%", fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aalhamadani/dataviz_final_project/main/figures/tpa_max_temps_density.png")
```

Hint: check the `kernel` parameter of the `geom_density()` function, and use `bw = 0.5`.

```{r, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.align='center', out.width="80%", fig.alt="Kernel density estimate of daily maximum temperatures in Tampa for 2022, using an Epanechnikov kernel with bandwidth 0.5. The distribution shows a bimodal shape with one peak around 65 degrees Fahrenheit (winter) and a larger peak around 90 degrees (summer)."}
tpa_clean %>%
  filter(!is.na(max_temp), max_temp != -99.9) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = max_temp)) +
  geom_density(bw = 0.5, kernel = "epanechnikov", fill = "grey40", color = "black") +
  labs(
    x = "Maximum temperature",
    y = "density"
  ) +
  theme_bw()
```

(c) Create a plot like the one below:

```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width="80%", fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aalhamadani/dataviz_final_project/main/figures/tpa_max_temps_density_facet.png")
```

Hint: default options for `geom_density()` were used. 

```{r, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.align='center', out.width="80%", fig.alt="Faceted density plots of daily maximum temperatures in Tampa for each month of 2022, colored with the viridis palette. Each panel shows the density distribution for one month, confirming narrower distributions in summer (concentrated heat) and wider spread in transitional months."}
tpa_clean %>%
  filter(!is.na(max_temp), max_temp != -99.9) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = max_temp, fill = month_name)) +
  geom_density(color = "black", show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~month_name, nrow = 3) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "viridis") +
  labs(
    title = "Density plots for each month in 2022",
    x = "Maximum temperatures",
    y = "density"
  ) +
  theme_bw()
```

(d) Generate a plot like the chart below:

```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width="80%", fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aalhamadani/dataviz_final_project/main/figures/tpa_max_temps_ridges_plasma.png")
```

Hint: use the`{ggridges}` package, and the `geom_density_ridges()` function paying close attention to the `quantile_lines` and `quantiles` parameters. The plot above uses the `plasma` option (color scale) for the _viridis_ palette.

```{r, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.align='center', out.width="80%", fig.alt="Ridgeline density plot of Tampa daily maximum temperatures by month for 2022 using the plasma viridis color scale. Months are stacked vertically with January at the bottom and December at the top. A vertical line marks the median for each month. The gradient from purple (cooler months) to yellow (hotter months) reflects seasonal temperature progression."}
library(ggridges)

tpa_clean %>%
  filter(!is.na(max_temp), max_temp != -99.9) %>%
  mutate(month_name = factor(month_name, levels = month.name)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = max_temp, y = month_name, fill = stat(x))) +
  geom_density_ridges_gradient(
    quantile_lines = TRUE,
    quantiles = 2,
    scale = 1.5
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
  labs(
    x = "Maximum temperature (in Fahrenheit degrees)",
    y = NULL,
    fill = NULL
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  scale_y_discrete(limits = month.name)
```

(e) Create a plot of your choice that uses the attribute for precipitation _(values of -99.9 for temperature or -99.99 for precipitation represent missing data)_.

```{r, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.align='center', out.width="80%", fig.alt="Bar chart of total monthly precipitation at Tampa International Airport in 2022. Florida's rainy season months (June through September) are highlighted in blue, while other months are grey. A bracket annotation labels the rainy season. July has the highest total at approximately 14.4 inches, labeled directly on the bar."}
# Calculate total monthly precipitation, excluding missing values
tpa_monthly_precip <- tpa_clean %>%
  filter(!is.na(precipitation), precipitation != -99.99) %>%
  group_by(month_name) %>%
  summarise(total_precip = sum(precipitation), .groups = "drop") %>%
  # Mark Florida's rainy season: June through September
  mutate(rainy_season = month_name %in% c("June", "July", "August", "September"))

# Find the peak month for annotation
peak_month <- tpa_monthly_precip %>% slice_max(total_precip, n = 1)

ggplot(tpa_monthly_precip, aes(x = month_name, y = total_precip, fill = rainy_season)) +
  geom_col() +
  # Annotate the rainy season with a bracket
  annotate("segment",
    x = 6, xend = 9, y = 16.5, yend = 16.5,
    color = "#1a6faf", linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  annotate("segment", x = 6, xend = 6, y = 16.0, yend = 16.5, color = "#1a6faf", linewidth = 0.8) +
  annotate("segment", x = 9, xend = 9, y = 16.0, yend = 16.5, color = "#1a6faf", linewidth = 0.8) +
  annotate("text",
    x = 7.5, y = 17.2,
    label = "Rainy Season", color = "#1a6faf", fontface = "bold", size = 3.5
  ) +
  # Label the peak month
  geom_text(
    data = peak_month,
    aes(label = paste0(round(total_precip, 1), '"')),
    vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold", color = "#1a6faf", size = 3.5
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("FALSE" = "grey70", "TRUE" = "#4da6e8"),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(
    title = "Total Monthly Precipitation — Tampa International Airport (2022)",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Total Precipitation (inches)"
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

**Comment:** Total precipitation was aggregated per month to accurately capture the seasonal pattern — an earlier per-event boxplot was misleading because April had only 7 rainy days, but each was a heavy isolated event, inflating its median. Summing totals gives the correct climatological picture.

Color is used purposefully rather than decoratively: only Florida's official rainy season (June–September) is highlighted in blue, drawing the reader's eye directly to the story. All other months are shown in grey to recede into the background. A bracket annotation reinforces the grouping without relying on the reader to infer it from color alone. The peak month (July, 14.4") is labeled directly on the chart, eliminating the need to estimate from the axis.

## Visualization (Interactive): Monthly Precipitation

The bar chart above is static. This interactive version lets the reader hover over each bar to see the exact total precipitation value and whether the month falls within Florida's rainy season — details that would require printed labels or axis estimation in the static version.

```{r interactive-precip, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.alt="Interactive bar chart of total monthly precipitation at Tampa International Airport in 2022. Rainy season months are blue, others grey. Hovering over each bar reveals the month name, total precipitation, and rainy season status."}
p_precip <- tpa_monthly_precip %>%
  mutate(
    label = paste0(
      month_name,
      "\nTotal: ", round(total_precip, 2), '"',
      "\nRainy Season: ", ifelse(rainy_season, "Yes", "No")
    )
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = month_name, y = total_precip, fill = rainy_season, text = label)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("FALSE" = "grey70", "TRUE" = "#4da6e8"),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(
    title = "Total Monthly Precipitation — Tampa International Airport (2022)",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Total Precipitation (inches)"
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

ggplotly(p_precip, tooltip = "text")
```


## PART 2 

### Option (B): Data on Concrete Strength 

Concrete is the most important material in **civil engineering**. The concrete compressive strength is a highly nonlinear function of _age_ and _ingredients_. The dataset used here is from the [UCI Machine Learning Repository](https://archive.ics.uci.edu/ml/index.php), and it contains 1030 observations with 9 different attributes 9 (8 quantitative input variables, and 1 quantitative output variable). A data dictionary is included below: 


Variable                      |    Notes                
------------------------------|-------------------------------------------
Cement                        | kg in a $m^3$ mixture             
Blast Furnace Slag            | kg in a $m^3$ mixture  
Fly Ash                       | kg in a $m^3$ mixture             
Water                         | kg in a $m^3$ mixture              
Superplasticizer              | kg in a $m^3$ mixture
Coarse Aggregate              | kg in a $m^3$ mixture
Fine Aggregate                | kg in a $m^3$ mixture      
Age                           | in days                                             
Concrete compressive strength | MPa, megapascals


Below we read the `.csv` file using `readr::read_csv()` (the `readr` package is part of the `tidyverse`)

```{r}
concrete <- read_csv("../data/concrete.csv", col_types = cols())
```


Let us create a new attribute for visualization purposes, `strength_range`: 

```{r}
new_concrete <- concrete %>%
  mutate(strength_range = cut(Concrete_compressive_strength,
    breaks = quantile(Concrete_compressive_strength,
      probs = seq(0, 1, 0.2)
    )
  ))
```



1. Explore the distribution of 2 of the continuous variables available in the dataset. Do ranges make sense? Comment on your findings.

```{r, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.align='center', out.width="80%", fig.alt="Histogram of cement content in the concrete dataset. Values range from approximately 100 to 540 kg per cubic meter. The distribution is right-skewed with most observations clustered between 200 and 400 kg per cubic meter, consistent with typical concrete mix designs."}
# Distribution of Cement
ggplot(concrete, aes(x = Cement)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#0072B2", color = "white") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Cement Content",
    x = "Cement (kg per m³)",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_bw()
```

```{r, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.align='center', out.width="80%", fig.alt="Histogram of water content in the concrete dataset. Values range from approximately 120 to 250 kg per cubic meter, consistent with standard water-to-cement ratios of 0.3 to 0.6. The distribution appears roughly unimodal."}
# Distribution of Water
ggplot(concrete, aes(x = Water)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#D55E00", color = "white") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Water Content",
    x = "Water (kg per m³)",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_bw()
```

**Comment:** Cement ranges from roughly 100 to 540 kg/m³, which is consistent with typical concrete mix designs — low cement mixes are used for lean concrete, while higher-cement mixes target high strength. The distribution is roughly right-skewed, with many observations clustering around 200–400 kg/m³. Water content ranges from about 120 to 250 kg/m³, which is realistic (a water-to-cement ratio of 0.3–0.6 is standard). Both ranges are physically plausible and align with known civil engineering practices.

2. Use a _temporal_ indicator such as the one available in the variable `Age` (measured in days). Generate a plot similar to the one shown below. Comment on your results.

```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width="80%", fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aalhamadani/dataviz_final_project/main/figures/concrete_strength.png")
```

```{r, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.align='center', out.width="80%", fig.alt="Grouped boxplot showing concrete compressive strength in MPa by curing age in days, with boxes colored by strength range quintile. At age 1 day, most samples fall in the lowest strength range. By ages 56 to 100 plus days, the highest strength range dominates, confirming that concrete strength increases substantially with curing time."}
new_concrete %>%
  filter(!is.na(strength_range)) %>%
  mutate(Age = factor(Age)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Age, y = Concrete_compressive_strength, fill = strength_range)) +
  geom_boxplot(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8)) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("tomato", "darkolivegreen3", "mediumseagreen", "cornflowerblue", "orchid"),
    name = "Strength Range"
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Age (in days)",
    y = "Compressive Strength (in MPa)"
  ) +
  theme_bw()
```

**Comment:** Concrete strength increases substantially with age, especially in the early curing period (days 1–28). This is expected — hydration of cement continues over time, increasing compressive strength. Samples in the highest strength range (magenta/orchid) become more prominent at later ages (56, 90, 91, 100+ days), while samples in the lowest strength range (red) dominate at age 1. The spread within each age group is large, reflecting the fact that strength depends heavily on the mix composition in addition to age.

3. Create a scatterplot similar to the one shown below. Pay special attention to which variables are being mapped to specific aesthetics of the plot. Comment on your results. 

```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width="80%", fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aalhamadani/dataviz_final_project/main/figures/cement_plot.png")
```

```{r, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.align='center', out.width="80%", fig.alt="Scatterplot of concrete compressive strength (y-axis) versus cement content (x-axis). Point size encodes curing age in days and point color encodes water content using the viridis palette (purple = low water, yellow = high water). Higher cement content and lower water content are both associated with higher compressive strength."}
ggplot(concrete, aes(
  x = Cement,
  y = Concrete_compressive_strength,
  size = Age,
  color = Water
)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "viridis") +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(0.5, 5), breaks = c(100, 200, 300)) +
  labs(
    title = "Exploring Strength versus (Cement, Age, and Water)",
    x = "Cement",
    y = "Strength",
    size = "Age",
    color = "Water",
    caption = "Age is measured in days"
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 1))
```

**Comment:** The scatterplot reveals several patterns: (1) Higher cement content is generally associated with higher compressive strength, as seen by points shifting upward as we move right along the x-axis. (2) Older samples (larger circles) tend to be stronger, confirming the age–strength relationship. (3) Water content (color) shows that lower water content (darker/purple tones) tends to correlate with higher strength, consistent with the well-known water-to-cement ratio principle in concrete technology — less water means a denser, stronger concrete.

## Before / After Redesign

Unlike Projects 01 and 02 — which are revisions of previously submitted work, improved based on course feedback — Project 03 is new. There is no prior submission to compare against. The before/after here instead documents a **design decision made during the development of this project**: the concrete boxplot (Part 2 Q2) was initially built with a manually chosen set of named R colors before being revised to apply the accessibility principles covered in this course.

The initial version used: `tomato`, `darkolivegreen3`, `mediumseagreen`, `cornflowerblue`, and `orchid` — a rainbow-style palette that looks varied but fails on two counts: the colors have no perceptual ordering (so the gradient from low→high strength reads as arbitrary), and several pairs are indistinguishable under deuteranopia (red-green colorblindness, the most common form).

**What was wrong:**

- `tomato` and `darkolivegreen3` are both warm-toned and easily confused under deuteranopia
- The palette has no perceptual ordering — there is no gradient to help the reader map low→high strength
- `orchid` and `cornflowerblue` are similar in lightness, making them hard to tell apart in print or greyscale

**Before:** The "before" chart is the boxplot already shown in Part 2 Q2 above with the rainbow palette. Since the only difference is the fill colors — data, geometry, axis labels, and layout are identical — reproducing the full chart here would only add redundancy. Scroll up to Part 2 Q2 to see it in context.

**After (redesigned):**

Switching to `scale_fill_viridis_d()` solves both problems: viridis is perceptually uniform (the purple→green→yellow gradient maps naturally to low→high), and it is safe for all major colorblindness types. The ordered palette makes the strength-range ranking visually self-evident without requiring the reader to mentally match legend swatches.

- Fill palette replaced with `scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "viridis")` — perceptually ordered, colorblind-safe
- `theme_bw()` replaces default styling for cleaner contrast
- Subtitle documents the palette choice explicitly

```{r redesign-after, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.align='center', out.width="80%", fig.alt="Redesigned grouped boxplot of concrete compressive strength by curing age. Strength range quintiles are now colored with the colorblind-safe viridis palette, progressing from purple (lowest range) to yellow (highest range). The perceptual ordering of the palette reinforces the low-to-high strength ordering."}
new_concrete %>%
  filter(!is.na(strength_range)) %>%
  mutate(Age = factor(Age)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Age, y = Concrete_compressive_strength, fill = strength_range)) +
  geom_boxplot(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8)) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "viridis", name = "Strength Range") +
  labs(
    title = "Concrete Compressive Strength by Age (Redesigned)",
    subtitle = "Viridis palette: perceptually ordered and colorblind-safe",
    x = "Age (in days)",
    y = "Compressive Strength (MPa)"
  ) +
  theme_bw()
```




## Discussion

### Part 1 — Tampa International Airport Weather (2022)

The temperature plots collectively paint a clear seasonal picture. Winter months (December–February) show wide, flat distributions spread across the 50s–70s °F range, while summer months (June–September) show narrow distributions concentrated in the high 80s to low 90s °F. This reflects Tampa's subtropical climate: summer temperatures are constrained by humidity and sea breeze, creating a kind of temperature ceiling, while winter temperatures vary more depending on cold front activity. The ridgeline plot makes this seasonal compression/expansion of distributions easy to see at a glance.

The precipitation chart highlights an important distinction between *frequency* and *total volume*. Some months have relatively few rainy days but heavy individual events (e.g. April), which can be misleading if visualized as a per-event boxplot. Summing monthly totals gives the correct climatological picture: June–September dominates, with July as the peak at approximately 14 inches. This is consistent with Florida's well-documented rainy season, driven by increased convective activity and sea-breeze fronts in summer.

### Part 2 — UCI Concrete Compressive Strength

The histograms confirm that input variable ranges are physically plausible: cement content (100–540 kg/m³) and water content (120–250 kg/m³) both fall within standard concrete mix design ranges. The right skew in cement content suggests that high-cement, high-strength mixes are less common in the dataset, which is typical of research samples covering a range of practical formulations.

The boxplot of strength by curing age shows a clear relationship: samples cured for only 1 day sit almost entirely in the lowest strength range, while samples cured 56+ days show substantially higher strength. The spread within each age group is large, reflecting the fact that mix composition (cement, water, admixtures) is as important as time. The scatterplot brings these factors together: higher cement content and lower water content are both associated with higher strength, and older samples (larger circles) cluster in the upper portion of the chart. These patterns align with the water-to-cement ratio principle, one of the most well-established relationships in concrete technology.

### Limitations

**Tampa weather data:** This analysis is limited to a single station (Tampa International Airport) and a single year (2022). Station data may not be representative of broader regional patterns, and a single year may not reflect typical climate if 2022 had anomalous weather events. Comparisons across years or stations would strengthen any climatological conclusions.

**Concrete dataset:** This is observational data from a research compendium, not a controlled experiment. The input variables are correlated with each other (e.g. higher cement content tends to come with lower water-to-cement ratios), which makes it difficult to isolate the independent effect of any single ingredient. A proper mixture experiment design would be needed to make causal claims about individual components.
